FACSIMILE: (202) 879-4899
TELEPHONE: (202) 879-4400

DALLAS
600 NORTH PEARL STREET, LB 183
DALLAS, TEXAS 75201
FACSIMILE: (214) 853-0038
TELEPHONE: (214) 853-0000

HONG KONG
2816-17 JARDINE HOUSE
1 CONNAUGHT PLACE
CENTRAL, HONG KONG
FACSIMILE: 852-5-868242
OR1852-5-284381

Ms. Carla Wardlow
Assistant Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Ms. Wardlow:

This letter is to confirm our telephone conversation
on January 22, 1991 regarding aggregation of contributions made
by a husband and wife. I understand that the Commission has
recently taken the position that a husband and wife are
considered separate donors and that contributions made from
jointly held funds are attributed to the spouse who signs the
check, unless an accompanying document indicates otherwise.

As I mentioned to you, I represent a husband and wife
who may be considered major donor committees. A number of
contributions made by our clients were from a family trust on
bank checks which have legends typed on them identifying the
contributor. The checks are neither imprinted with the name of
either spouse nor signed by either of them. Based on our
telephone conversation, I understand that the trust is treated
as an agent of the husband and wife and that a contribution from
the trust is attributed to the spouse who directed that the
contribution be made. I further understand that the identifying
legends on the trust checks and enclosure letters to candidate
committees (which are not signed by either spouse) provide
sufficient evidence to attribute the contribution to a
particular spouse.

If this letter does not accurately describe our
telephone conversation, please contact me.

Sincerely yours,

[signature]

LAWRENCE B. LOW

LBL:cmc
cc: Mr. Wayne Imberi

embedding.qa_text: This letter is from the law firm Pettit & Martin, confirming a telephone conversation with Ms. Carla Wardlow of the Fair Political Practices Commission regarding the aggregation of contributions made by a husband and wife. The Commission has taken the position that a husband and wife are considered separate donors, and contributions made from jointly held funds are attributed to the spouse who signs the check, unless an accompanying document indicates otherwise. The letter explains that the firm represents a husband and wife who may be considered major donor committees. Contributions made by their clients were from a family trust on bank checks which have legends typed on them identifying the contributor. The checks are neither imprinted with the name of either spouse nor signed by either of them. Based on the telephone conversation, it was understood that the trust is treated as an agent of the husband and wife and that a contribution from the trust is attributed to the spouse who directed that the contribution be made. It further understands that the identifying legends on the trust checks and enclosure letters to candidate committees (which are not signed by either spouse) provide sufficient evidence to attribute the contribution to a particular spouse. If this letter does not accurately describe the telephone conversation, please contact the author. Sincerely yours, Lawrence B. Low LBL:cmc cc: Mr. Wayne Imberi
